nativeRange.habitat: Can inhabit any reef habitat; tolerates a range of depths and light levels. Colonies have been documented in shallow lagoons and on reef slopes to approximately 50 m depth.

decorPreferences: stable base with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented benefiting greatly from target (spot) feeding 1–2 times per week with meaty frozen foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cyclops, and zooplankton. Liquid and pellet foods have also been reported as accepted. Polyps extend nocturnal feeding tentacles.

avoidWith: other corals placed within sweeper range (3–7 cm)
other Acanthastrea colonies without buffer spacing

summary: Acanthastrea echinata (the 'true Acan') is a beginner-friendly LPS coral found across the Indo-Pacific. Known for vivid color morphs and mild sweeper tentacles, it suits middle-to-lower placement with moderate flow and weekly target feeding.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note: The trade name 'Acan' and 'Acan Lord' historically referred to multiple species in the genus Acanthastrea. Since the 2016 reclassification described by Arrigoni et al. (based on morphological and molecular data), Acanthastrea lordhowensis was split into Micromussa lordhowensis (the former 'Acan Lord'), while the genus Acanthastrea was reduced to eight recognized species: A. echinata, A. bowerbanki, A. hillae, A. brevis, A. hemprichii, A. minuta, A. pachysepta, A. rotundoflora, and A. subechinata. A. echinata (Dana, 1846) is now considered the primary 'true Acan' in the aquarium trade. A. hemprichii was additionally reclassified to Homophyllia australis in the same body of work. The trade still applies 'Acan' generically to both Acanthastrea spp. and Micromussa lordhowensis.

Key care notes:
• Lighting: Moderate PAR (75–200 PAR) is generally recommended. Sources suggest an optimal range of approximately 100–250 PAR, with 150 PAR as a reasonable midpoint target. Excessive intensity has been reported to damage tissue; acclimate new specimens by placing lower in the tank and gradually moving to the final position over 2–4 weeks.
• Flow: Low to moderate, indirect flow is preferred. Direct high flow has been reported to cause polyp retraction and tissue tears. Gentle alternating flow keeps the coral clean without stressing tissue.
• Placement: Middle to lower areas of the aquarium are generally advised, away from direct current impact and high-intensity light zones.
• Aggression: Sweeper tentacles typically extend 3–7 cm (approximately 1.5–3 inches) at night and are capable of stinging neighboring corals. A minimum buffer of 10 cm (~4 inches) from other specimens is commonly advised, with more space recommended as the colony grows. Aggression has been noted even between two Acanthastrea colonies.
• Feeding: Spot feeding 1–2 times per week is considered well-suited to this species. Meaty frozen foods target-fed via pipette or turkey baster are reported to promote growth and coloration. The coral appears to benefit from moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–25 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.2 ppm); ultra-low nutrient systems may cause color loss.
• Care level: ReefChasers rates this species 'moderate' partly due to slower growth rates and historically limited aquaculture availability. Many reef hobbyists and trade sources classify it as beginner-to-intermediate LPS given its hardiness relative to SPS corals. The beginner designation applies within a correctly cycled reef system with stable water chemistry.
• Colony size: Wild colonies have been documented reaching approximately 1 m across. In home aquaria, typical growth produces colonies of 10–40 cm depending on tank size and conditions, with slow but steady expansion over years.
• Colors: Wild specimens are often dull gray, brown, or green; aquaculture and trade specimens frequently exhibit vivid multi-colored polyps (reds, oranges, greens, blues) due to selective acquisition of colorful morphs. These designer color morphs are still sold under the generic 'Acan' trade name.
• Fragging: Colonies can be fragged with a bandsaw or bone cutters; frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs. Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity.
• Do not confuse with Micromussa lordhowensis (formerly 'Acan Lord'), which belongs to family Mussidae and is now classified under a separate genus. Both are beginner-friendly LPS corals with overlapping care requirements, but they are biologically distinct.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) is the primary propagation method in captivity and is widely practiced by hobbyists. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw; frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs; deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: ReefChasers – Acan Coral Care Guide (Acanthastrea echinata)

additional.name: Wikipedia – Acanthastrea echinata
additional.notes: Used for native range (western Indian Ocean through Pacific to southeastern Atlantic), depth range (to 50 m), colony size (up to ~1 m), and habitat description. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Acanthastrea (genus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order: Scleractinia, Family: Lobophylliidae), confirmed current 8-species genus composition following reclassification events. Confirmed A. lordhowensis is no longer in Acanthastrea.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Micromussa lordhowensis reclassification article
additional.notes: Used to document the Arrigoni et al. reclassification split: A. lordhowensis → Micromussa lordhowensis. Confirmed remaining Acanthastrea species include A. echinata, A. bowerbanki, and A. hillae (core trade-relevant species).

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Aquacultured Aussie Lord Frag (Micromussa lordhowensis)
additional.notes: Used for context on trade nomenclature overlap and to cross-reference care parameters for the former 'Acan Lord' (now Micromussa lordhowensis). Temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025. These parameters are for M. lordhowensis, not A. echinata; used only to confirm overlapping salinity/kH ranges. LiveAquaria carries no current A. echinata-specific product page.
